meta.title: Web Worker vs main thread for heavy browser compute
meta.summary: A browser app parses large local data, builds a search index, and recomputes facets while the UI must stay responsive. Would you keep that work on the main thread or move it to a Web Worker?

scenario: You are building a browser-based analytics tool. Users can drop a 25 to 50 MB CSV file into the page, and the app parses it locally, normalizes rows, builds a searchable index, computes summary facets, and then lets users type filters while scrolling a large result list. The team wants the app to feel responsive on mid-range laptops, including while the file is first processed and while filters are recomputed. One engineer wants to keep everything on the main thread because that is simpler and avoids message-passing complexity. Another wants to move parsing and heavy computations into a Web Worker so input, scrolling, and paint stay responsive. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Keep the work on the main thread
options.summary: Fits best when the computation is light, tightly coupled to DOM work, or too small to justify worker coordination overhead.
whenItWins: The work is short enough that it does not noticeably block input, paint, or scrolling on realistic devices.
The code needs tight coordination with layout, DOM measurement, or immediate UI updates that workers cannot perform directly.
The complexity of message passing, cancellation, and data transfer would outweigh the actual compute cost.
watchOutFor: Once parsing or recomputation starts causing typing lag, dropped frames, or scroll jank, the 'simpler' answer can become the more expensive product choice.
A weak answer stays on the main thread because it feels easier, without checking whether the browser's UI thread is now doing too much CPU work.

options.label: Move heavy compute into a Web Worker
options.summary: Fits best when CPU-heavy browser work would otherwise compete with input, scrolling, and paint on the main thread.
whenItWins: Parsing, indexing, filtering, or transformation work is large enough to block the main thread and hurt responsiveness.
The heavy work can be modeled as data processing, while DOM updates and final rendering still stay on the main thread.
The product needs to keep typing, scrolling, progress updates, and cancellation responsive while background compute continues.
watchOutFor: Workers are not free. Transfer cost, serialization, progress reporting, and cancellation handling all need to be designed well.
A weak answer says 'just use a worker' without explaining which boundaries stay on the main thread and how results move back safely.

decisionMatrix.title: Small or infrequent compute
decisionMatrix.prompt: The transformation work is modest and does not create measurable UI jank on target devices.

cells.note: This is easier to defend when the work is genuinely cheap and worker coordination would add more moving parts than value.
Still possible, but now you need a stronger reason than 'workers are faster' because the main bottleneck may not exist.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: Large local files are parsed and indexed in the browser, while typing and scrolling should stay responsive on mid-range devices.

cells.note: Harder to defend because CPU-heavy parsing and recomputation will compete directly with the UI thread.
This is easiest to defend because the product is explicitly sensitive to responsiveness during heavy compute.

decisionMatrix.title: Work tightly coupled to layout or direct DOM access
decisionMatrix.prompt: The task frequently depends on measuring DOM, updating layout, or using browser APIs that only the main thread can use.

cells.note: This becomes easier to defend because the worker cannot own the DOM-facing part of the workflow anyway.
A worker can still help with some sub-compute, but it is no longer the clean default if the core loop is DOM-bound.

evaluationDimensions.title: Will this work steal time from input, paint, or scrolling?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. If the same CPU work runs on the main thread, it competes directly with the browser's visible responsiveness.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is the job mostly data processing or mostly DOM-coupled work?
evaluationDimensions.description: Workers are strongest when the heavy part is pure compute over data rather than work that must touch layout or the DOM.

evaluationDimensions.title: What does it cost to move data across the thread boundary?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should consider serialization, transfer size, batching strategy, and whether the work can reuse worker-owned data instead of constantly copying it.

evaluationDimensions.title: How will you handle cancellation, partial progress, and stale results?
evaluationDimensions.description: Once you introduce background compute, you should explain how the UI stays correct when the user changes filters mid-flight or loads a new file.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is the added architecture worth operating?
evaluationDimensions.description: Workers improve isolation, but they add protocol design, debugging, testing, and bundling complexity. A senior answer should price that in.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean Web Worker because the prompt is explicitly CPU-heavy and responsiveness-sensitive
strongAnswer.summary: This analytics tool is doing substantial local parsing and recomputation while the UI must remain responsive. That makes a Web Worker the easier answer to defend for the exact prompt.
reasoning: The key signal is not just that the data is large. It is that parsing, indexing, and facet recomputation happen in the same experience where the user is typing, scrolling, and expecting responsive feedback.

If heavy compute stays on the main thread, it directly competes with input handling and paint. Moving the data-processing portion into a worker protects the UI thread so the app can still render progress, accept cancellation, and keep interactions smooth.

I would keep DOM ownership, virtualization, and final rendering on the main thread, but move CSV parsing, indexing, and filter recomputation into a worker with explicit request ids so stale results can be ignored or cancelled.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would use a Web Worker for the heavy data-processing pipeline and keep the main thread focused on UI orchestration, rendering, progress state, and final result application. I would stay on the main thread only if profiling showed the work was actually light or too DOM-coupled to isolate cleanly.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not keep it simple first and optimize later if it becomes slow?
interviewerPushback.answer: I would absolutely start with measurement, but this prompt already tells me the workload is large and responsiveness matters on mid-range devices. That makes thread isolation part of the initial design, not just a premature optimization. I would still keep the worker boundary narrow and only offload the CPU-heavy pipeline.

interviewerPushback.question: Do workers automatically solve performance problems?
interviewerPushback.answer: No. They solve UI-thread contention, not every kind of inefficiency. You still need to care about algorithm cost, transfer size, batching, caching, and stale-work cancellation.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If profiling showed the compute was actually small, infrequent, or heavily tied to DOM measurement, I would stay on the main thread because the worker boundary would buy less and cost more.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would use a Web Worker because heavy work should never run on the main thread.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds absolute and ignores coordination cost, DOM boundaries, and cases where the work is too small to justify another thread.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably use a worker because parsing a large file could block the UI.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a clearer explanation of what stays on each thread and how you would handle stale or cancelled work.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would move CSV parsing, index building, and filter recomputation into a Web Worker because those are CPU-heavy data tasks that would otherwise compete with typing, scrolling, and paint on the main thread. I would keep virtualization, DOM rendering, and progress UI on the main thread, and I would send worker requests with ids so stale filter results can be ignored if the user changes inputs again. If profiling later showed the workload was actually small or tightly coupled to DOM measurement, I would collapse back to the main thread to avoid unnecessary coordination overhead.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it explains the product risk, draws a clear thread boundary, and shows that the answer is based on workload shape rather than worker dogma.

answerFramework: Start by asking whether the heavy part is CPU-bound data processing or DOM-coupled UI work.
Explain what would happen to typing, scrolling, and paint if that work stays on the main thread.
Choose the execution model that matches the real bottleneck.
Describe the boundary clearly: what stays on the UI thread, what moves to the worker, and how results come back.
End with the condition that would flip your answer, usually around measured cost, transfer overhead, or DOM coupling.
antiPatterns: Saying workers are always better without pricing in transfer and coordination cost.
Keeping obviously CPU-heavy browser work on the main thread because it feels simpler.
Ignoring cancellation, stale results, or progress reporting once background compute exists.
Treating DOM work and data processing as if they can move to a worker equally easily.
